title: Regression and The Error Term
one: Everything your model leaves out doesn't vanish — it goes into the **error term**, and "close all the back doors" translated into regression means exactly one thing: **make your treatment unrelated to the error term**, because if it isn't,
text: Everything your model leaves out doesn't vanish — it goes into the error term , and "close all the back doors" translated into regression means exactly one thing: make your treatment unrelated to the error term , because if it isn't, OLS hands your treatment the credit for whatever is hiding in there. Residual = actual minus predicted, for a data point. OLS picks the line making the squared residuals smallest. Error term (ε) = everything in the data generating process your model left out. Different concept. The error term is not noise . It's real determinants of your outcome — parents' wealth, lottery wins — that simply aren't in your equation. Adding a control variable moves it out of the error term into the model . That's what controlling is , in regression. The translation: closing all back doors ≈ making X unrelated to the error term. If X is related to it: omitted variable bias , X is endogenous , the effect isn't identified — all the same statement. Why it biases: OLS can't see Z, but it sees X hanging around while Z does good things. So it credits X. Part 2 of the book starts here — more technical, more about applying tools rather than thinking about identification in the abstract. And it starts with regression, because regression is enormously important and enormously common in causal inference. You already met it in Chapter 4. Regression estimates a conditional relationship by picking a shape — often a straight line — and fitting the best version of that shape. You can go further than a line into a plane in 3D space by adding control variables, and that easy flexibility to add controls is a big part of why it's so popular: it lets you isolate just the part of the X–Y relationship not explained by the control. So what's left to say? This: you've been focusing on the line you fit. Now focus on what you left out . Residual vs. error — two different things Plain English. A residual is how wrong you were about one point. The error term is what your whole model ignores about the world. Precisely. Residual — the difference between your prediction and the actual value, written as actual minus prediction. Some points sit above the line (reality was higher than you predicted), some below. Any prediction method has residuals, and they arise for many reasons: things left out, sampling variation, whatever. Error term — the difference between what your model would predict if it had all the data in the world and you had the best possible version of that estimated model , and what would still be left over. It captures only one thing: what's in your model versus what's still missing from it. Anchor image. A weather forecast. The residual is that it said 20° and it was 22°. The error term is that the model never looks at the ocean. Worked example. Predicting someone's wealth from their income. Higher paying job, more wealth accumulated — probably a strong relationship. Residual: you predict wealth from income, and sometimes you're a bit high, sometimes a bit low, for any individual. Error term: if income is the only thing you use, then everything else that predicts wealth — your parents' wealth, winning the lottery — is not in your model. It's still real, it still determines the outcome, and it has to go somewhere. That somewhere is the error term. Where it breaks — this is the important bit. The error term is not just random noise . Writing the data generating process as y = β₀ + β₁x + ε makes two claims: that x is in the data generating process for y, and that there's other stuff too. If you're predicting wealth from income, it isn't that everything besides income is noise — you can name several of those things, and they're real determinants of wealth. The linguistic jump: controlling = moving things out of the error term Plain English. A control variable doesn't create information. It relocates it. Precisely. Start with y = β₀ + β₁x + ε . If z is part of the data generating process for y — if z was used in the creation of y — then z is already in that first model . You can't see it, but everything determining y is in there somewhere, and if it isn't x, it's in ε. Now add z as a predictor: y = β₀ + β₁x + β₂z + ε . You haven't discovered z. You've moved z out of the error term and into the model. Anchor image. A room where the mess is either on the shelves or under the bed. Putting something on a shelf doesn't create it — it just stops being part of the unexamined pile. Why it matters: this changes the relationship between x and the error term. If z and x are related, then in the first model x is related to the error term . In the second, you've removed the part of the error term x was related to — and maybe now x is unrelated to it. The jump. We've spent chapters saying close all the back doors to identify the effect of X on Y . In a regression context, that is very closely related to saying: Make X unrelated to the error term. Omitted variable bias, and its many names Plain English. Leave out something correlated with your treatment, and your treatment absorbs its effect. Precisely. If x is related to the error term you have omitted variable bias . You may also hear that x is endogenous . These, plus "the effect isn't causally identified" and "you haven't closed all the back doors", are all largely the same statement. The "omitted variable" part: you've omitted from your model a variable related to x. Because it's omitted it lives in the error term, so x and the error term are related, and your estimate won't reflect the actual causal effect of x on y. Anchor image. A colleague who is always standing next to whoever fixes things. You never see the fixer. You promote the colleague. Worked example — why the bias happens. You estimate the relationship between y and x, and there's a z out there. Suppose z is positively related to x and positively related to y , but you fit the first model, so z sits in the error term. Z is doing all sorts of positive things for y. OLS doesn't know z exists — you n

title: Regression and Sampling Variation
one: An OLS coefficient is **normally distributed across samples**, centred on the population parameter, with a spread — the **standard error** — driven by how well your model predicts, how much your X varies, and how many observations you have;
text: An OLS coefficient is normally distributed across samples , centred on the population parameter, with a spread — the standard error — driven by how well your model predicts, how much your X varies, and how many observations you have; and hypothesis testing uses that distribution to ask whether your starting assumption survives, which is a much narrower question than people treat it as. Set identification aside. You don't have all the universe's data, so your estimate carries sampling variation . Across samples, an OLS coefficient follows a normal distribution , centred on the population parameter . Its standard deviation is the standard error , and three things drive it: model prediction error σ (up top), the variance of X , and the sample size n (both below). All three are about information . More information, more precision. Hypothesis testing: assume a null, build the sampling distribution it implies, see how unlikely your result was. Either something unlikely happened, or the assumption was wrong. It does not tell you the probability your estimate is correct. Nick's rant: the null is arbitrary, significance depends on sample size, insignificance can be the right answer, and never change your model to chase stars. You're doing statistics, which means you don't have all of the universe's data. The estimate you got isn't a perfect representation of the truth — ideally it's a representation of the truth plus some sampling variation and noise built in. So you've run a regression and got a coefficient. What does that estimate actually mean? To answer that you need to know how it would have bounced around if you'd drawn different data. The sampling distribution of an OLS coefficient Plain English. Collect data, estimate, repeat forever, and plot all the estimates. You get a bell curve centred on the truth. Precisely. An OLS coefficient follows a normal distribution across samples — provable, though not proved here. Its mean is the population parameter : what you'd get if you did have all the data in the universe and estimated your model. So on average, OLS gives you the population parameter. Anchor image. A well calibrated archer. Individual arrows scatter; the centre of the scatter is where they aimed. Worked example. You estimate 2.5. You don't know the population parameter — maybe it's 2.5, maybe 3, maybe 2. But you can say the truth is probably not 10 . That's the whole game: using the distribution to make claims about the population parameter from your single estimate. Where it breaks. "On average OLS gives you the population parameter" is not the same as giving you what you want. That population parameter can itself be biased if you haven't got causal identification. This episode is only about sampling variation. The standard error and its three ingredients Plain English. The standard error is how wide the bell curve is. Three things make it narrow, and all three are versions of "you have more information." Precisely. A standard error is the standard deviation of a sampling distribution. For an OLS coefficient it has three components: Component Where What it means σ — prediction error numerator The variance of the error term, estimated by the variance of the residuals. How closely your model can predict the outcome. Variance of X denominator How much your predictor moves around. Sample size n denominator How many observations you have. σ — model precision. If your model predicts the outcome very accurately, you know a lot about what's going on, so you can state your parameter with real confidence. Small residuals → small σ → small standard error. Knowing a lot about the outcome means your coefficient won't vary much from sample to sample. Variance of X. You can say more about a variable's effect if it moves around a lot — and the intuition is straightforward. If X moves a lot and Y stays still, you know nothing is happening. But if X barely moves and Y doesn't change much either, maybe you just can't see it. It's simply hard to study things with statistics that don't vary. More movement along X makes it far easier to see whether there's movement along Y. Sample size. More observations, more information, more precision. A large sample gives a small standard error because you have a lot to build inferences from. Anchor image. Reading a sign in fog. It gets clearer if the fog thins (σ), if you can walk back and forth to see it from angles (variance of X), or if a hundred people read it and compare (n). Why statisticians care about distributions at all Plain English. Knowing how a thing varies lets you rule out claims about the truth. Worked example. You estimate an OLS coefficient of 2 . Could that have come from a distribution with population parameter 100 and standard deviation 1? Essentially never — you'd basically never see a value that low. And yet you did. So that's the wrong sampling distribution. Hypothesis testing, carefully Plain English. Assume something, work out what data you'd expect, and see whether what you got is absurd under that assumption. Precisely. The procedure: 1. Make an assumption about the world — the null hypothesis . Say the true population parameter is 100. 2. Build the sampling distribution that assumption implies. You know it's normal, you know it's centred on the assumed parameter, and you know the standard deviation — so you know exactly what the sampling distribution would be, and how likely any particular estimate is under it. 3. Compare your actual estimate to it. 4. If your outcome was very unlikely under that distribution, exactly two things could have happened: something very unlikely just occurred , or your starting assumption was wrong . Worked example. Null hypothesis: the average person is 5 feet 7 inches tall. That gives you a sampling distribution of sample averages centred on 5'7", varying a bit above and below from sample to sample. Now you draw a sample and it averages 3 feet . Under the null, that is wildly unlikely from random chance alo

title: Reading Regression Tables
one: Every cell in a regression table is the same three things — **estimate**, **standard error in parentheses**, **stars for the p-value** — and the fit statistics underneath (R², adjusted R², F) describe the model as a whole, which for causal
text: Every cell in a regression table is the same three things — estimate , standard error in parentheses , stars for the p value — and the fit statistics underneath (R², adjusted R², F) describe the model as a whole, which for causal work you should mostly ignore. A regression table is compact and lets you compare models side by side. Each column is one regression. Rows are the predictors, plus an intercept (a.k.a. constant, β₀, α). Each cell: estimate , then in parentheses a standard error — or sometimes a t statistic — then significance stars . Stars encode the p value against a default null of coefficient = 0 , and they stack . What the stars mean varies by field. Look for the table note. Below: number of observations , then model fit statistics — R² , adjusted R² , F statistic . For causal inference, R² is mostly irrelevant. A clean randomized experiment often has a small R² and a very good model. After that rant, something straightforward. A regression table is just a table with regression results on it — a very common presentation because it's compact, easy to read, and lets you show several models at once, all read the same way. The layout Plain English. Columns are models. Rows are variables. The stuff at the bottom is about the model, not any one variable. Worked example. Two regressions predicting restaurant health inspection scores : (1) (2) Intercept … … Number of locations −0.019 <br (0.000) … Year of inspection −0.065 <br (0.006) Observations 27,178 … R² 0.065 … The top names the dependent variable — what you're predicting, your y. Here it's inspection scores in both columns. The first row is the intercept , which some tables call the constant ; in an equation it's β₀ or α. Then the predictors — the independent variables, your x and your controls. Here: number of locations (for chain restaurants) and year of inspection. Column 1 uses only number of locations; column 2 includes both. Reading a single cell Plain English. Three numbers stacked: what you estimated, how precise it is, how surprising it is under a null of zero. Worked example. Take −0.019 in column 1. 1 · The estimate. The first number is the estimated coefficient. If you wrote out y = β₀ + β₁ × number of locations , this is your β₁ hat — the estimated slope on number of locations is −0.019. 2 · The parentheses. Below sits 0.000 , a standard error . Some tables instead show a t statistic — the coefficient divided by the standard error. A table note usually says which; if not, here's the heuristic: Significant coefficients with small values in parentheses → standard errors Significant coefficients with large values in parentheses → t statistics And 0.000 doesn't mean the standard error is actually zero — it can't be. It means it's smaller than the smallest value the table is willing to display. 3 · The stars. Those asterisks are significance stars , representing the p value. By default in essentially all regression tables, the null being tested is that the coefficient is zero . So: assume the slope on number of locations is zero, build a sampling distribution centred on zero with a standard error of (here) roughly zero, and ask how unlikely it would be to see an estimate of −0.019 or further away in either direction. That probability is the p value, and the stars encode it. In this table — the standard convention in economics: Stars Meaning p < 0.1 p < 0.05 p < 0.01 Other fields commonly use for 0.05, for 0.01, for 0.001. Look for the table note. Three stars therefore means p < 0.01 — significant if you'd chosen a 0.01 cutoff. And you can read it in reverse: had you picked 0.01 as your threshold, this coefficient clears it. Stars stack , because if p < 0.01 then it's also below 0.05 and 0.1. Column 2's year of inspection cell reads the same way: estimate −0.065 , standard error 0.006 , p < 0.01. Where it breaks. Depending on how the table is built, standard errors sometimes sit beside the coefficient rather than below. That's fine — just a formatting choice. Model fit statistics Plain English. Numbers at the bottom describing the whole model rather than any coefficient. Number of observations. Here, 27,178. R². The correlation between your model's predicted values and the actual outcome, squared. It represents the proportion of the outcome's variance explained by the model. With R² = 0.065: doing 1 − 0.065 = 0.935 says that if you split the outcome into the part your model predicted and the part left in the residual, 6.5% of the variation is explained and 93.5% is not . It partitions the outcome into in the model and out of the model. Where R² breaks — and it gets misused a lot. R² measures only how much variance you explained. It does not tell you how good the model is. For causal work you want to estimate one parameter very well. The concern is whether −0.019 reflects the causal effect of number of locations on inspection score. That plenty of other things drive inspection scores simply doesn't matter to you. Never change your model to raise R². The clinching example: a randomized controlled experiment has very clean, very easy causal identification, and often a small R², because a lot is going on in the outcome that the randomly assigned treatment doesn't explain. R² looks bad; the model is excellent. If your only goal is prediction , R² makes more sense — that's what it's about. Even then, there are probably better measures. Overall, in this book, you can more or less ignore R². It's a nice quick diagnostic with a sharp interpretation — the share of outcome variation inside versus outside your model — but that's all it does, and it isn't answering a question you care about. Adjusted R². R² will always go up as you add variables, because more predictors means more flexibility to fit the data — and that's true even if the variables you add are complete nonsense. Adjusted R² instead asks whether the variables you included explain more than you'd expect from adding random noise . Nick's view: he uses adjusted R² less

title: Interpreting a Regression Coefficient
one: There is one sentence — *a one-unit change in X is associated with a coefficient-sized unit change in Y* — and it handles essentially every regression coefficient you will ever meet, provided X appears in only one place in your model.
text: There is one sentence — a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y — and it handles essentially every regression coefficient you will ever meet, provided X appears in only one place in your model. The mantra: a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. Say it out loud. Fill in the blanks. Done. With controls: adjusting for all the other predictors, a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. "Associated with", not "increases". Causal language requires causal identification, which by default you don't have. If X is transformed — say you regressed on log(X) — the sentence still works. It's just about the log, so you have extra work to get back to X. It only fails when X appears more than once in the model: polynomials (X and X²) or interactions (X, Z, X×Z). When it fails, use the derivative instead. The effect becomes an expression, not a single number. This is genuinely simple, and people work far too hard to make it complicated for themselves. If you have a regression model you're unsure how to interpret, the fix is almost always to say the sentence again and slot the pieces in: what's X, what's Y, what does a one unit change mean here? The mantra Plain English. One unit up in X, coefficient many units up in Y. Precisely. If you regress Y on X and get a coefficient: A one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. Add controls and it barely changes: Adjusting for all the other predictors in the model, a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. Anchor image. A currency conversion rate. One of these buys that many of those. The coefficient is the exchange rate between X units and Y units. Where it breaks. This always works as long as X only shows up in one place in your model. Wrinkle 1 — "associated with" is doing deliberate work Plain English. The mantra is written in non causal language on purpose. Precisely. "Associated with" implies no causal relationship, and that's the correct default — you shouldn't be assuming you've causally identified anything. You may have heard the causal phrasing: a one unit change in X increases Y by the size of the coefficient. That version assumes causal identification. If you genuinely have it, you're entitled to say "increases" or "decreases". Otherwise, "associated with". Anchor image. The difference between "these two arrive together" and "this one brings the other." Wrinkle 2 — transformed variables Plain English. If you regressed on a transformation, the sentence describes the transformation, not the original variable. Precisely. Regress Y on log(X) and the mantra still applies exactly: a one unit change in the log of the predictor is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. The sentence never breaks, because you're just regressing an outcome on a predictor. Where it breaks. You probably don't care about a one unit change in the log of X — you care about X. So the sentence works, and then you have some work to do afterwards converting it into a statement about the thing you're actually interested in. That's the next episode. Wrinkle 3 — when the sentence genuinely fails Plain English. If X appears twice, you can't change one X without changing the other. Precisely. The mantra fails in one common setting: X shows up in more than one place among the predictors. Two ways that happens: Polynomials — regressing Y on X and X² Interaction terms — regressing Y on X, Z, and X×Z Why it breaks: the full mantra says holding all the control variables constant , a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized change in Y. But you can't hold the others constant . If your model contains X and X², you cannot change X by one unit without also changing X². They move together. The fallback: the derivative Plain English. The coefficient was always a slope. When there are several, compute the slope directly. Precisely. Another interpretation of a regression coefficient: it's the derivative . If you regress Y on X, then the derivative of Y with respect to X is simply β₁. "A one unit change in X is associated with a certain sized change in Y" is describing the slope of the line. For a more complex model — Y on X and X², or anything else — do the same thing: take the derivative of Y with respect to X. You'll get a calculation, which may itself contain coefficients and variable values. Whatever that expression is, that's the relationship between X and Y: A one unit change in X is associated with a [calculation] sized unit change in Y. Anchor image. A single coefficient is a fixed exchange rate posted on the wall. A derivative is a rate that depends on how much you're changing — you have to look it up for your amount. A coefficient means X increases Y by that amount → Actually, "associated with" unless you've established causal identification. The causal phrasing is a separate claim. The mantra needs modifying for models with control variables → Actually, you just prepend "adjusting for all the other predictors". The structure is identical. If I regressed on log(X), the coefficient tells me about X → Actually, it tells you about a one unit change in log(X). Getting back to X takes extra work. The mantra breaks whenever the model gets complicated → Actually, it breaks in one specific circumstance: X appearing in more than one place. I can hold X² constant while changing X → Actually, you can't. That's precisely why the mantra fails for polynomials and interactions. Derivatives are a separate, more advanced interpretation → Actually, it's the same interpretation. A single coefficient is the derivative; multi place X just makes it an expression. People tie themselves in knots interpreting regression coefficients, and honestly it's one sentence. Here it is: A one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. That's it. If you're staring at a model you do

title: Polynomials in Regression
one: Regression gives you the best version of the shape you chose — and the best version of a bad shape is still bad — so when a relationship curves, add powers of X to let the line bend, then accept that the effect of X is now **different at ev
text: Regression gives you the best version of the shape you chose — and the best version of a bad shape is still bad — so when a relationship curves, add powers of X to let the line bend, then accept that the effect of X is now different at every value of X and read it off with a derivative. Regression fits the best version of the shape you picked . Pick badly and you get the best version of something bad. Default OLS shape: a straight line. Plenty of relationships aren't straight. Fix by transforming variables so the transformed relationship is straight. The two big ones in causal inference: polynomials and logarithms . A polynomial adds powers of X. Linear = first order. Quadratic = X and X². Cubic = X, X², X³. Keep the lower terms. Diagnose the need by plotting residuals against X . A visible pattern is a warning sign. Stop at third order. More gets weird — don't add them willy nilly. Interpretation: there is no single effect of X , and there shouldn't be. Take the derivative and plug in a value of X. Remember what regression is: a shape fitting procedure. You supply a shape and it finds the best version of that shape for your data. Getting the best version of a shape sounds great — but you still had to pick the shape, and if the shape doesn't make sense for your data you get back a bad result. Even the best version of a bad thing is still a bad thing. By default, ordinary least squares fits a straight line. Look at data that clearly curves: fit a straight line and you'll get the best possible line, which is still a poor fit, because the relationship simply isn't well described by a straight line. Transformations Plain English. You can't bend the line, so bend the variables instead. Precisely. If the relationship between your variables isn't a straight line, you can often still get a good fit from regression by transforming the variables — so that while the original data has no straight line relationship, the transformed data does. Several transformations are popular in statistical analysis; the book covers a few, and there are more it doesn't. The two you'll meet most in causal inference and regression analysis are polynomials and logarithms . Polynomials Plain English. Add X squared to let the line curve. Add X cubed to let it curve back. Precisely. A polynomial adds additional powers of the variable to your model. Model Terms What it allows Linear — first order polynomial X A straight line. Slope never changes: increase X by one and Y always moves by β₁. Quadratic — second order X and X² One curve. Parabola shaped, though zoomed in it may not curve back on itself. Cubic — third order X, X², X³ A curve, then a curve back the other direction. Quartic, quintic, … X⁴, X⁵, … More wiggle than you want. Keep the lower order terms. A quadratic includes X and X². You don't leave the X out. Anchor image. A garden hose. Straight by default. One bend, two bends — after about three you're not shaping it, you're just tangling it. Where it breaks. You're pretty much always fine with the first three orders. Beyond that, don't get wild: too many polynomial terms makes the model behave strangely, and you shouldn't add them willy nilly. Diagnosing with residuals Plain English. Plot your residuals against X. If they make a shape, your line has the wrong shape. Precisely. With a straight line fit to curved data, the residuals sit consistently above the line at low X, dip below in the middle, then come back above at high X. That's a relationship between the residuals and X, which you don't want. Why it matters: one of the assumptions you need about the error term is that it's unrelated to X . What you're really worried about is the error term, not the residuals — but a visible pattern in the residuals is still not a good sign. Worked example. Plot the residuals from both fits of the same curved data: Straight line model: a clear pattern remains — residuals shift systematically as you move along the X axis. Quadratic model: no relationship whatsoever between residuals and X. Exactly what you want. Where it breaks. So the rule is: plot residuals against X, and if you see a pattern, that may indicate you want a polynomial term to get rid of it. Interpreting polynomial terms Plain English. You can't read one coefficient anymore — and there is no single effect of X. Precisely. Once X appears in more than one place, you must consider all the places it appears to interpret its effect. And stranger still: there is no single effect of X, and there shouldn't be. The effect of a one unit increase in X is the slope of the fitted line, and in a polynomial model the slope is changing all the time. So the effect differs at low, middle, and high values of X. How to get it: write out the equation and take the derivative. For a cubic model, the derivative of Y with respect to X is: dY/dX = β₁ + 2·β₂·X + 3·β₃·X² where β₁ is the coefficient on X, β₂ on X², and β₃ on X³. Notice X is in that expression. The whole expression is the effect of X — so to state an effect you must pick a specific value of X to evaluate it at. At X = 0 : everything else drops out, and the effect is just β₁ . At X = 1 : the effect is β₁ + 2β₂ + 3β₃ . Worked example: chain restaurants Worked example. Regress restaurant health inspection scores on the number of locations the chain has, with a quadratic term: Predictor Coefficient Number of locations −0.08 Number of locations² 0.0001 You want: comparing two chain restaurants where one has one more location than the other, what's the association with the inspection score? Write out the model and take the derivative: Score = −0.08 · locations + 0.0001 · locations² + … dScore/dLocations = −0.08 + 2 · 0.0001 · locations Now plug in whatever value of X you care about: At this many locations The effect of one more location 1 −0.08 + 2(0.0001)(1) = −0.0798 1,000 −0.08 + 2(0.0001)(1,000) = +0.12 Going from one location to two, and going from a thousand locations to a thousand and one, give completely different answ

title: Logarithms in Regression
one: Logarithms fix two problems at once — they scrunch skewed variables so billionaires don't drag your line around, and they convert additive changes into **proportional** ones — at the cost of an interpretation step, which you can do exactly
text: Logarithms fix two problems at once — they scrunch skewed variables so billionaires don't drag your line around, and they convert additive changes into proportional ones — at the cost of an interpretation step, which you can do exactly or with the approximation that log(1 + small) ≈ small . Reason 1 — skew. In social science, a few observations are many times bigger than the mass. OLS minimises squared residuals, so it chases the billionaire. Reason 2 — proportional relationships. Some relationships should multiply rather than add. Interest rates don't add dollars to your wealth, they multiply it. Logs keep the big values in the data but scrunch them , so they still count without over influencing. Logs also give proportional interpretations, because log(x) + log(y) = log(x·y) turns addition into multiplication. The mantra still works — it's just about a one unit change in the log , so you need a conversion step. Approximation: log(1 + a) ≈ a for small a. Breaks down fast — good to about 10%, useless at 50%. Logs can't take zero or negative values, and zeros are a real problem. Polynomials let a straight line curve. Logarithms solve two different problems, and they get enormous use in the social sciences because we deal with a lot of highly skewed variables. Remember the framing: it's fine to fit a straight line between some non straight things . That's what lets you model non straight relationships with a procedure that only draws straight lines. Reason 1: skew and over influential outliers Plain English. A few observations are astronomically bigger than everyone else, and OLS contorts itself to accommodate them. Precisely. A skewed variable has a mass of relatively similar observations and a few that are way different. Crucially, it isn't just that there are outliers — it's that the outliers are so many times bigger than the mass. Worked example. Wealth. A typical person has a few thousand dollars, maybe tens of thousands, maybe a hundred thousand depending on the group. Then there's someone with billions and billions. Why OLS struggles: it minimises the sum of squared residuals. Suppose it's describing the mass of people well — and then there's someone whose wealth it's off by billions on. That residual is enormous, so the model changes to fit that one person well. Anchor image. A see saw with a hundred children on one end and a cargo ship on the other. It doesn't matter how many children there are. Where it breaks — the honest nuance. Outliers aren't necessarily wrong data, and they should affect your analysis — they're part of your data. The problem is only that you don't want them to have undue, over large influence on your model. That's really what the concern about outliers is. Also worth noting: in the social sciences the skew is almost always in this direction. There aren't many variables where a lot of people are very large and a few are very, very tiny. Reason 2: relationships that should be proportional Plain English. Some things multiply rather than add, and a straight line can't express that. Worked example. Your wealth over time depends on what's in your savings and the interest or investment return on it. Regress wealth on investment return — that shouldn't be a straight line relationship. If the return rate goes up by a certain percentage, your wealth doesn't rise by a certain number of dollars ; it rises by a certain percent . If the interest rate goes up by 0.01, that should multiply your wealth by 1.01, not add a fixed amount. What logs do about both Plain English. They squash the giants without deleting them, and they turn plus into times. Precisely. Skew: logs take those very large values and keep them in the data but scrunch them down, so there's less distance between them and the mass. They're still counted; they just don't pull as hard. Proportionality: logs have a convenient proportional interpretation, because log(x) + log(y) = log(x·y) . The mechanism. A regression gives you additive changes — "a one unit increase in X is associated with a coefficient sized increase in Y." But adding 1 is the same as adding log(e¹) . And log(x) + log(e) is log(x·e) . So an additive one unit increase in the log becomes a multiplication of the original x by e . That's how a linear increase becomes a proportional one. Interpreting: the exact way Plain English. Push the coefficient back through the log algebra until you get a percentage. Precisely — two directions. Log on the left: regress log(y) on x. a one unit change in x → β₁ unit change in log(y) β₁ = log(e^β₁) [natural logs, always, in social science] log(y) + log(e^β₁) = log(y · e^β₁) So y has increased proportionally by e^β₁ . Log on the right: regress y on log(x). What's the effect of a 1% increase in x? a 1% increase means x · 1.01 log(x · 1.01) = log(x) + log(1.01) so log(x) rose by log(1.01) units therefore y rises by β₁ · log(1.01) Where it breaks. That's the accurate route: convert the linear change your regression gives you into a proportional change you can actually interpret — in either direction. Interpreting: the approximation Plain English. For small numbers, log(1.05) is basically 0.05. So percentages and log units are nearly the same thing. Precisely. log(1 + a) ≈ a when a is small: Expression Approximately log(1.01) 0.01 log(1.05) 0.05 log(1.1) 0.1 Where it breaks — quickly. This is an approximation and it degrades fast as the change grows. You would not say a 50% increase in x (a 1.5 proportional increase) is about a 0.5 increase in log(x). That no longer works. Keep the change around 0.1 (10%) or lower ; above that, do it properly. Worked example — three walkthroughs. Start from the mantra and convert one stage at a time. Regress y on log(x): 1. A one unit change in log(x) is associated with a β₁ sized change in y. 2. Scale everything down by 100 so the approximation applies: a 0.01 unit change in log(x) is associated with a 0.01·β₁ change in y. 3. A 0.01 unit change in log(x) is approximately a 1% change

title: Categorical Predictors
one: A binary predictor in a regression is just a **comparison of means**, and a categorical variable is a set of binary indicators with **one left out as the reference group** — which means every coefficient, and every significance test on it,
text: A binary predictor in a regression is just a comparison of means , and a categorical variable is a set of binary indicators with one left out as the reference group — which means every coefficient, and every significance test on it, is relative to whichever category you happened to omit. A binary predictor compares means . Regress income on treatment, get 5,000: mean income is 5,000 higher among the treated. Same as a two sample t test. Why bother with regression? Because you can add control variables . It follows from the mantra: with a binary variable the only sensible one unit change is 0 → 1. The intercept becomes the mean of the group coded zero. A categorical variable becomes one binary indicator per category — and you must leave one out . The omitted category is the reference group . Every other coefficient is a difference from it . Change the reference group and every coefficient and every significance test changes . To test the variable as a whole, use a joint F test . You've been working on the right hand side of a regression — transformations, non linear terms. Now something you'll see constantly: predictors that are categories. Lots of variables are like this. Did you get the treatment or not — you can't half get it. Which continent are you on — seven mutually exclusive options, and you can't be on two at once (barring something odd at a border). So how do you put those on the right hand side, whether as your treatment or as a control? A binary predictor compares means Plain English. The coefficient is the gap between the group averages. Precisely. Including a binary predictor gives you a comparison of means — exactly the same comparison as a two sample t test. Anchor image. A step, not a slope. There's only one place to put your foot down. Worked example. Regress income on whether you got the treatment, and the coefficient comes back 5,000 . That says the mean income is 5,000 higher for the group that got the treatment than for the group that didn't. Why use regression instead of a t test? Because you can add control variables. Then you can say: controlling for everything I need to identify my effect, the effect of the treatment on the outcome is [coefficient] — the mean of the outcome rises by that much going from 0 to 1. You also get a standard error and a significance test on the coefficient for free, letting you check whether the average outcome differs statistically between the yes group and the no group. Why it works. Return to the mantra: a one unit increase in X is associated with a coefficient sized change in Y. Now make X binary. There's only one one unit change that makes any sense at all — going from 0 (not in the group) to 1 (in the group) . So the coefficient gives the jump in the outcome you'd see, on average, going from a no to a yes. The intercept becomes the reference mean Plain English. Set the binary variable to zero and only the intercept is left — so the intercept is the mean of that group. Worked example. Regress sales on whether it's currently winter. Average sales are 10 in winter and 15 in not winter . Term Coefficient Why Intercept 15 Set winter = 0 and only the intercept remains, so it must be the not winter average Winter −5 Winter sales are 5 lower than not winter Your model should give the best prediction it can — the winter average for winter, the not winter average for not winter. That's exactly what these two numbers deliver. Where it breaks — the key idea. The coefficient on winter is relative to something : winter versus not winter. A binary coefficient is always about being relative to something else. That's the bridge to categorical variables with more than two categories. Categorical variables: one indicator per category, minus one Plain English. Split the categories into yes/no variables, then throw one away. Precisely. Take each category and turn it into its own binary indicator. For continents: are you in Asia, yes or no? In Europe, yes or no? In South America, yes or no? And so on — seven categories, seven binary indicators. Then, when you run the regression, you have to leave one out. The reason is statistical (the book has the full explanation), but you do have to. Whichever you leave out becomes your reference group . A note on vocabulary. You may have heard binary indicators called dummies . Nick avoids the term — if you're talking to someone who doesn't know econometrics and you say "I put the female dummy in the model," that may not go over well. Binary indicator is the better term. Where it breaks. People in the omitted group are still in your data. They just don't get their own coefficient — they become the intercept, and everything else is measured relative to them. Leave out Asia, and the coefficient on Europe is not "the effect of being in Europe." It's the difference in means between Europe and Asia. And South America's coefficient is South America versus Asia. And Africa's is Africa versus Asia. Everything is relative to the same reference group. Because of this, the individual coefficients don't mean much by themselves. They only mean something relative to the reference group you selected. The reference group is arbitrary — and so are your significance tests Plain English. Pick a different omitted category and every number in the table changes. Worked example. Income regressed on country, with three countries in the data: Country Average income France 30 Gambia 30.5 New Zealand 33 Reference group: France. Term Coefficient Why Intercept 30 France's average — the prediction when both other indicators are 0 Gambia 0.5 30.5 − 30 New Zealand 3 33 − 30 Now switch the reference group to Gambia. France re enters the model with its own term: Term Coefficient Why France −0.5 30 − 30.5 New Zealand 2.5 33 − 30.5 Where it breaks. All the coefficients — and therefore all the statistical significance tests — are completely arbitrary, determined by whichever reference group you happened to choose.
